content.full_text:
April 25, 1990

Ms. Jeanne Pritchard
Chief of the Technical Assistance and Analysis Division
California Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Dear Ms. Pritchard:

We regularly practice before the California Public Utility Commission (CPUC). The CPUC's telecommunications proceedings traditionally were by hearing with a formal transcript. However, the CPUC has changed its practice.

The CPUC's principal current means of issue resolution is though a process called the "workshop". The process is quite analogous to a traditional prehearing conference, but on a broader scale. The workshop is based on a formal notice and meeting date, and the proceeding is chaired by an ALJ or CPUC staff member. The difference from a hearing is there is no transcript, only secretarial notes, and discussion is not sworn testimony. There is usually a round of briefs, called comments, and a report from the CPUC staff that goes to the Commissioners recommending potential action.

No one of whom we are aware considers this practice to be lobbying under your rules. However, it has come to our attention that because of these procedural differences from a traditional hearing, which the CPUC now rarely uses for telecommunications issues, participation in the workshop process might be considered lobbying.

We request you issue a ruling of general application as to whether or not this CPUC required process constitutes lobbying under your rules. In the interim we will, of course, continue to report as lobbying all ex parte contacts in such proceedings with Commissioners or their staff under your commonly used definition.

Ms. Jeanne Pritchard  
April 25, 1990  
Page Two

of such activity. Additionally, our records would allow us to supplement our reports and add the workshop information should you decide that is appropriate.

Very truly yours,

Alan J. Gardner

AJG:nm
